---
title: "How do I get permission to save change to file /dev/fstab"
date: 2009-03-13
forum: General Help
---

### Post by ozguitarplayer on 2009-03-13
I need to make a correction to file /etc/fstab

the line.......# Entry for /dev/ !! UNKNOW DEVICE !! :
should read....# Entry for /dev/Doze :

In Nautilus when I try to save the change I am told that I do not have permission.

# /etc/fstab: static file system information.
#
#  -- This file has been automaticly generated by ntfs-config -- 
#
# <file system> <mount point>   <type>  <options>       <dump>  <pass>

proc /proc proc defaults 0 0
# Entry for /dev/sda2 :
UUID=6fb141e4-c570-41b3-9160-e99f4c5de138 / ext3 relatime,errors=remount-ro 0 1
# Entry for /dev/ !! UNKNOW DEVICE !! :
UUID=7ac19c1c-df27-48ef-b7cc-7f507606e1bf none swap sw 0 0
/dev/scd0 /media/cdrom0 udf,iso9660 user,noauto,exec,utf8 0 0
/dev/scd1 /media/cdrom1 udf,iso9660 user,noauto,exec,utf8 0 0
/dev/fd0 /media/floppy0 auto rw,user,noauto,exec,utf8 0 0
/dev/sdb1 /media/Doze ntfs-3g defaults,locale=en_AU.UTF-8 0 0

In Ubuntu Documentation - Introduction to fstab it states;

If a device/partition is not listed in fstab ONLY ROOT may mount the device/partition. 

which means that I have to manually mount/unmount the /dev/Doze partition
in terminal each time I need to access the partition.

So I need to be root to mount/unmount the partition, which I can do yet I don't have permission to make changes to correct the file.

How do i correct the permission issue so I can save the changes?

---

### Post by estyles on 2009-03-13
> **ozguitarplayer said:**
> So I need to be root to mount/unmount the partition, which I can do yet I don't have permission to make changes to correct the file.

How do i correct the permission issue so I can save the changes?

Simple - you also need root permissions to modify /etc/fstab.  Otherwise, it wouldn't make much sense to restrict drive mounting if you could still mount anything you want.

Just use "sudo nano /etc/fstab" or "gsku gedit /etc/fstab".  (Always use gksu instead of sudo when using a GUI program or it could cause issues.)

---

In Nautilus when I try to save the change I am told that I do not have permission.

# /etc/fstab: static file system information.
#
#  -- This file has been automaticly generated by ntfs-config -- 
#
# <file system> <mount point>   <type>  <options>       <dump>  <pass>

proc /proc proc defaults 0 0
# Entry for /dev/sda2 :
UUID=6fb141e4-c570-41b3-9160-e99f4c5de138 / ext3 relatime,errors=remount-ro 0 1
# Entry for /dev/ !! UNKNOW DEVICE !! :
UUID=7ac19c1c-df27-48ef-b7cc-7f507606e1bf none swap sw 0 0
/dev/scd0 /media/cdrom0 udf,iso9660 user,noauto,exec,utf8 0 0
/dev/scd1 /media/cdrom1 udf,iso9660 user,noauto,exec,utf8 0 0
/dev/fd0 /media/floppy0 auto rw,user,noauto,exec,utf8 0 0
/dev/sdb1 /media/Doze ntfs-3g defaults,locale=en_AU.UTF-8 0 0

In Ubuntu Documentation - Introduction to fstab it states;

If a device/partition is not listed in fstab ONLY ROOT may mount the device/partition. 

which means that I have to manually mount/unmount the /dev/Doze partition
in terminal each time I need to access the partition.

So I need to be root to mount/unmount the partition, which I can do yet I don't have permission to make changes to correct the file.

How do i correct the permission issue so I can save the changes?

A line starting with # is a comment. So it really doesn't matter what that line says, it doesn't have any effect on how anything is mounted.

If you really do feel you want to change it, you can use sudo to temporarily get root privileges to make the change. See [uwiki]RootSudo[/uwiki] for details.

---

### Post by heindsight on 2009-03-13
> **ozguitarplayer said:**
> Thanks heindsight,
I'm new to this....
Do you mean that even if I can change the line it wont solve the mounting issue?

Exactly, it's a comment so changing it won't change anything.
Looking at your fstab file, it seems to me that that comment relates to your swap device (which you shouldn't be mounting anywhere anyway).

All filesystems listed in /etc/fstab (except ones explicitly marked as noauto) are automatically mounted when the machine boots and should generally remain mounted until you shut down again.

I don't really understand why you would need to manually mount or unmount anything. Perhaps I misunderstand your problem though. Could you please describe exactly what the problem is?

EDIT:
I read your original post again and I think I understand a bit better now. You have some partition which is not being mounted? You mention /dev/Doze, but that's not a proper partition name, partitions usually have names like /dev/sda1 or /dev/sdb1. I do notice that you have a /dev/sdb1 wich is being mounted at /media/Doze, is that perhaps the partition you're having problems with?

Perhaps you should run the following commands in a terminal and post the output here:
```
sudo blkid
mount
```
The first will give a list of all your partitions, together with some information about each. The second will list all mounted filesystems. 

With that information we can see what partitions you have available, what is mounted where and what you may need to add to /etc/fstab to get everything working the way you want it...

### Post by heindsight on 2009-03-15
OK, what you could do is to modify the line for /dev/sdb1 in your fstab from
```
/dev/sdb1 /media/Doze ntfs-3g defaults,locale=en_AU.UTF-8 0 0
```
To
```
UUID=2000247000244EDA /media/Doze ntfs-3g defaults,users,locale=en_AU.UTF-8 0 0
```
That way, /media/Doze will be mounted automatically when you boot. All files in /media/Doze will belong to user root and group root, and everybody will have full read, write, execute and directory browsing permissions. (al least that's what it says in ntfs-3g manual -- i've never used ntfs-3g myself). That should be how it has been up to now anyway, however, we've added the **users** option in fstab which means that any user can unmount the filesystem and also any user can mount it again thereafter (if it is unmounted and remounted, the files will belong to the user who mounted the filesystem).

You could also add the noauto option:
```
UUID=2000247000244EDA /media/Doze ntfs-3g defaults,noauto,user,locale=en_AU.UTF-8 0 0
```
Now it will not be mounted at boot, but any user can mount it (however since the **users** is changed to **user** here, only the user who mounted the filesystem can unmount it). As before, all files will belong to the user/group who mounted the filesystem but everyone will have full access.

Another option is to do something like:
```
UUID=2000247000244EDA /media/Doze ntfs-3g defaults,uid=1000,gid=1000,fmask=033,dmask=022,locale=en_AU.UTF-8 0 0
```
This way, The filesystem will be mounted at boot, and only root will be able to unmount it again. The files and directories will belong to the user with **uid** 1000 (in ubuntu this is the first user created when you installed) the files will belong to the group with **gid** 1000 (the group with the same name as the first user). The **fmask=033** option means owner will have read and write permissions on all files (everyone else will only have read access). The **dmask=022** option means the owner will have read, write and browsing permissions for directories and everyone else will only have read and browsing permission.

Of course you can also combine the **uid**, **gid**, **fmask** and **dmask** options with the **noauto** option (to prevent mounting at boot) and/or with either of the **user** or **users** options (you can't have both **user** and **users** at once - they contradict each other).

I hope this helps. Try reading the manuals for fstab, ntfs-3g and mount for more information (a lot of what I've posted here comes straight out of those manuals):
```
man fstab
```
```
man mount
```
```
man ntfs-3g
```

Of course if you need more help, you're welcome to post back here and I'll be happy to try to give you more assistance...

EDIT:
By the way, I notice you have FAT32 filesystems labeled Niles and Spare on /dev/sda6 and /dev/sda2, which weren't mounted when you ran the mount command and have no mention in fstab. If you'd like, I could try to help you add fstab entries for those too...

---

### Post by SuperSonic4 on 2009-03-15
Were you in Krusader root mode or user mode? If I recally korrectly (;)) then root krusader has a red icon whereas user's is grey. 

If you want to use kate (replace kate with kwrite if you prefer that editor): ```
kdesu kate /etc/fstab
```

Personally I like nano which is the main cli editor which would be ```
sudo nano /etc/fstab
```

If you want to back it up first (always wise) then ```
sudo cp -v /etc/fstab /etc/fstab.bak
```

just for my own interests are you running ubuntu or kubuntu?

You don't need both the /dev/sdb1 and the UUID on the same line

### Post by ozguitarplayer on 2009-03-15
will it make a difference if they are not on the same line, and where would I seperate the lines...after the UUID=2000247000244EDA?

---

### Post by heindsight on 2009-03-16
> **ozguitarplayer said:**
> will it make a difference if they are not on the same line, and where would I seperate the lines...after the UUID=2000247000244EDA?

You only need one of either UUID=2000247000244EDA or /dev/sdb1

I used the UUID in the examples I gave because I think it's better to use UUID's in fstab rather than device names. If you prefer the device name then remove the UUID=2000247000244EDA and just keep the /dev/sdb1.

If you decide to use the UUID, you may want to add a comment (a line starting with #) above it, just to remind yourself which device the following line is for.

---

```
